The goal of a self-driving vehicle is to take sensor observations of the environment.
The goal of a self-driving vehicle is to utilize high-definition maps for executing safe and comfortable plans.
It is important for a self-driving vehicle to produce interpretable representations that explain its maneuvers.
Traditional autonomy stacks break down the problem into 3 tasks: perception, motion forecasting, and motion planning.
Perception leverages sensor data to localize the traffic participants in the scene.
Motion forecasting outputs the distribution of future motion of traffic participants.
Motion planning is tasked with deciding which maneuver the self-driving vehicle should execute.
Most autonomy systems are object-based, involving detecting the objects of interest in the scene.
Object detectors use predicted confidence scores to detect objects of interest in the scene.
Object detectors threshold predicted confidence scores to determine which objects in the scene, a trade off between precision and recall.
Object-based motion forecasting methods are limited to predict only a handful of sample trajectories or parametric distributions with closed-form likelihood for tractability.

edge: Object-based motion forecasting methods scale linearly with the number of objects and must run online in the vehicle.
Object-free approaches have emerged to address the shortcomings of object-based models.

The spatio-temporal grid is a 3-dimensional dense grid with two spatial dimensions representing the bird’s-eye view, and a temporal dimension from the current observation time to a future horizon of choice.
IMPLICITO utilizes an implicit representation to predict both occupancy probability and flow over time directly from raw sensor data and HD maps.
IMPLICITO enables downstream tasks such as motion planning to efficiently evaluate a large collection of spatio-temporal query points in parallel.
IMPLICITO focuses on areas of interest where there are potential interactions with the self-driving vehicle.
IMPLICITO is designed with an architecture that overcomes the limited receptive field of fully convolutional explicit architectures by adding an efficient yet effective global attention mechanism.

The implicit network is trained by minimizing a linear combination of an occupancy loss, which is calculated using binary cross entropy loss between predicted and ground truth occupancy.

The flow loss is supervised to predict the motion of a query location when it is occupied, using ℓ2 error and backwards flow targets computed as rigid transformations between consecutive object box annotations.

The number of offsets (K) influences the occupancy-flow prediction. The attention offset visualizations suggest that the predicted attention offsets play a role in achieving state-of-the-art performance.
The predicted attention offsets of the implicit decoder contribute to its state-of-the-art performance.
Ablating the number of predicted offsets (K) affects the occupancy-flow prediction. K = 0 predicts occupancy-flow from zq alone without cross-attention.
K = 1 outperforms K = 0, especially on HwySim. The attention offsets expand the receptive field of a query point, leading to improved predictions.
K = 1 exhibits the best occupancy prediction metrics compared to other numbers of attention offsets.
K = 4 performs the best in some flow metrics. Having more attention offsets improves performance when occupancy could come from multiple past locations.

We have proposed a unified approach to joint perception and prediction using a neural network that implic- itly represents occupancy and flow over time with a neural network.
Our implicit architecture predicts occupancy and flow more accurately than contemporary explicit approaches in both urban and highway settings.
Our implicit architecture outperforms more traditional object-based perception and prediction paradigms.
In the future, we plan to assess the impact of our improvements on the downstream task of motion planning.
